verdicts.reasoning:
I re-read all primary sources and re-ran the probe in my own worktree. The finding's *mechanics* reproduce exactly; its *conclusion* does not survive, on three independent grounds.

**1. The probe reproduces — and, extended by one case, refutes the framing.** My own probe (`/private/tmp/.../scratchpad/probe/main.go`, implementing all three D4 arms with real `os.Stat`/`os.SameFile`, run via `mise exec -- go run`):
```
Case 1 hardlink -> ~/.netrc (protected root itself)
  d4Rejects = true                      <- S==P arm catches it
Case 2 hardlink -> ~/.ssh/id_rsa (inside protected dir ~/.ssh)
  d4Rejects(S, ~/.ssh) = false          <- as the finding says
  readable via alias: "PRIVATE KEY BYTES\n"
Case 3 mount ~/src/project (a plain allowlisted DIR, no alias as source)
  d4Rejects(proj, ~/.ssh) = false
  the same key bytes are inside it: true
```
Case 3 is the one the finding did not run, and it is decisive: mounting the ordinary allowlisted directory exposes the identical bytes with **no hardlink involved at all** and with no D4 arm implicated. So the exposure is not a property of D4's lineage walk; it is the property that a mount policy classifies addresses, not contents. Removing the hardlink from the scenario does not remove the exposure — which means the hardlink was never the cause.

**2. That behaviour is ADR-017's own explicit, deliberate decision — re-litigating it is out of ADR-021's scope.** `docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md:229`: *"These classify the requested mount **address**, not the recursive contents of an ordinary Worksource. A workspace may intentionally contain its own `.env` tool secret (§5)"*. And it is a *named rejected alternative*, `:1355-1356`: *"Recursively banning blocked names inside a Worksource contradicts §5's intentional Worksource tool-secret visibility."* The code already states the same law: `mc/boundary/blocked.go:96-97` — *"It classifies only address components; it never scans directory contents."* Content-reachability inside an allowlisted root is a decided, documented, intentional property of ADR-017, not a gap ADR-021 left. ADR-017 wins over ADR-021 on conflict, and ADR-021 may only be judged on what ADR-017 left open.

**3. D4 does not make the claim the finding attributes to it, and hardlinks are not in ADR-021's mandate.** Verbatim `docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md:144-149`: *"For canonical source `S` and protected root `P`, reject when `S == P`, `S` is a descendant of `P`, **or `S` is an ancestor of `P`**. Every comparison is `os.SameFile` on resolved objects — never a string prefix."* The words "aliasing" and "complete answer" do not appear. D4 states (i) a three-armed containment rule and (ii) that the comparison primitive is identity rather than string. Both are true, and (i) is a verbatim pin of ADR-017:388-389 (*"A source intersecting a protected root in either direction rejects"*). A hardlink to a file inside `~/.ssh` does not make the source *intersect* `~/.ssh` in either direction — under ADR-017's rule it is genuinely not an intersection, so D4 is a faithful, not a defective, pin. Further, ADR-021's mandate is the nine gaps (a)-(i) in `docs/reviews/2026-07-14-adr-017-jurisdiction-extract.md:203-260`; I read all nine and none concerns hardlinks. ADR-021:9-10 says it *"adds nothing to ADR-017's policy"* — adding a hardlink rule would violate its own charter.

**4. The ADR-017:361 citation cuts the other way.** *"Historical pipeline-trace entries are regular-file hardlinks governed only by Decision 7; they are never independently mounted."* This is ADR-017 handling hardlinks in the one place they are a mechanism and explicitly walling them off from the ordinary mount path. It does not establish that Decision 5 owes hardlink handling; it shows Decision 7 already owns them.

**5. Reachability.** The finding honestly concedes the model cannot create the link. Beyond that, Case 3 shows the pre-existing host-side link is not even load-bearing — an operator whose allowlisted workspace contains credential bytes has exposed them by §5's design, hardlink or not. No invariant is broken, no fail-closed posture is weakened, nothing is unimplementable.

**Calibration check (I confirm the finding's non-findings).** `os.SameFile("/Users/vinchenkov", "/System/Volumes/Data/Users/vinchenkov")` and the APFS case-fold pair both return true in my probe environment's equivalent — D4's identity rule does settle the routes it claims. That part of the finding's work is sound and I found nothing wrong with it.

What survives is at most a footnote — "an inode with a second lineage outside a protected root is not reachable by any lineage walk; per ADR-017:229/:1355 that is the address-not-contents rule, deliberate" — and even that belongs to ADR-017's Decision 2, not ADR-021's D4. That is not a defect in ADR-021.
verdicts.recommended_action: Do not act on this against ADR-021. No change to D4 is required, and adding a hardlink rule would breach ADR-021's own charter ("adds nothing to ADR-017's policy", :9-10) and reverse an explicitly rejected ADR-017 alternative (:1355-1356). If the reviewer's residual documentation instinct is worth honouring at all, the cheapest correct home is one clause in ADR-021 D6's existing "sharp edges" prose — which already exists to warn readers that jurisdiction's surface is narrower than it looks — noting that jurisdiction classifies the mount address, so an inode reachable by a second lineage inside an allowlisted root is out of scope by ADR-017:229 and :1355. Optional, info-grade, and explicitly not a blocker for the TDD slice. Do not add a hardlink test to D4's list: it would pin a behaviour ADR-017 decided elsewhere and would imply D4 owns a rule it does not.

verdicts.reasoning:
I re-read both primary sources verbatim in my own worktree rather than trusting the offered evidence.

WHAT IS TRUE: The cite is off-by-one on both ends. `awk 'NR>=374 && NR<=382' docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md` confirms line 376 is the tail of the *sessions* bullet ("separate typed projection, never a session source as an ordinary mount;") and the MC_HOME enumeration bullet actually runs :377-381. ADR-021:116 cites ":376-380". That is a genuine, if cosmetic, citation error.

WHY THE FINDING NONETHELESS FAILS — three independent checks, each fatal:

1. The failure scenario is NOT reachable. Its mechanism is that a reader following the cite "sees a clean fifteen-class list with no exception." I rendered the exact cited range (`awk 'NR>=376 && NR<=380'`); its LAST line (:380, in range) reads verbatim: "including the allowlist, except each exact typed own-source grant in". The exception clause is inside the cited range. Only its cross-reference tail ("Decisions 4, 6, and 8;") falls outside. The finding's own title says the cite "truncates the exception clause at :380-381" — but :380 is cited. No reader of :376-380 can conclude "no exception" when the range terminates in the word "except". The scenario requires a state the text cannot produce.

2. The claim "The dropped :380-381 exception clause is the only thing in Decision 5 that authorizes typed own-source grants inside MC_HOME" — on which the finding rests its escalation from partial to "total" — is false. `grep -n "^## Decision" docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md` shows Decision 5 spans :364-610. ADR-017:396-401 therefore sits INSIDE Decision 5 and independently authorizes the same grants: "A typed system mount is instead confined to its one kind-specific authorized root: the exact own session, derived own state, selected runtime-control directory, or generated projection may be inside `MC_HOME`, but any sibling/ancestor/other identity is still denied." ADR-021 cites that passage twice — :38 ("the typed-system exception at :349-353 and :396-401") and :371 (a required test citing ":396-401"). The carve-out is neither exclusive to :380-381 nor lost by ADR-021.

3. The "adds no member ADR-017 does not already imply" rebuttal attacks an argument D3 does not make. D3:116-119 grounds whole-tree protection on ADR-017:345 ("complete `MC_HOME`" is absent) and phase3-contract.md:178, NOT on the enumeration. Its clause "every enumerated class is inside the tree" is a true statement about the enumerated classes offered as secondary support. Moreover ADR-021 Sharp Edge 2 (:325-330) openly declares the strictness ("That is the intended direction of failure ... it is deliberate, and this line is the warning"), so nothing is silently dropped — which is precisely what AGENTS.md §6 and the deviation rule ask for.

SCOPE: the finding also partly re-litigates ADR-017's own structure — whether the union's exception clause governs typed mounts at all is answered by ADR-017:396 ("That union governs ordinary/profile-requested mounts. A typed system mount is instead confined to...") and :353 ("that exception cannot be requested as an ordinary profile mount"), i.e. upstream ADR-017 text already makes an ordinary mount claiming a typed own-source grant unconstructible.

Code grounding: `mise exec -- go build ./...` passes in my worktree; identity.go:228-230 confirms the reserved seat ("Protected-root and cross-Worksource jurisdiction (step 5) ... are NOT applied here") and enclosesByIdentity (identity.go:198-210) walks one direction as D4 describes. Nothing in mc/boundary bears on this textual finding either way.

ADJACENT REAL DEFECT (not this finding, must not be used to confirm it): `grep -n "^### D" docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md` shows D5 is "HOME is injected, not ambient". Yet D3:137 and Sharp Edge 2:328 both route the typed-grant carve-out to "D5's per-class check". No decision in ADR-021 defines a per-class typed check, and D1's `Rejects(id SourceIdentity)` takes only device/inode/type — no way to express "typed session source claiming its own session". That is a separate, likely more serious finding about a dangling reference and an unspecified carve-out mechanism. It should be raised on its own evidence, not merged into this one.
verdicts.recommended_action:
Do not accept this finding as written. Its failure scenario is unreachable (line 380, which contains "except each exact typed own-source grant in", is inside the cited range) and its load-bearing "only thing in Decision 5" claim is false (ADR-017:396-401 is in Decision 5 and ADR-021 cites it twice, at :38 and :371).

Residue worth a trivial fix, info-level only: correct ADR-021:116 from "ADR-017:376-380" to "ADR-017:377-381" so the cite spans the whole bullet including "Decisions 4, 6, and 8;". One-character-class edit, no design impact.

Separately — and this is where the reviewer's instinct was pointing, but it needs its own finding with its own evidence: ADR-021 D3:137 and Sharp Edge 2:328 both refer to "D5's per-class check" for the typed-grant carve-out, but D5 (:179) is "HOME is injected, not ambient" and no decision in ADR-021 specifies a per-class typed check. Combined with D1's `Rejects(id SourceIdentity)` carrying only device/inode/type, ADR-021 gestures at the carve-out ADR-017:396-401 requires without defining a mechanism that can express it. Raise that as a distinct finding against D1/D3 before TDD begins; it is checkable and plausibly major, whereas the citation off-by-one is not.

verdicts.reasoning:
I rebuilt the probe from scratch rather than trusting the reported one, and reproduced the result exactly.

TEXT (verified verbatim, my own reads):
- ADR-021:383-384: "**`Authorize` grows a fourth parameter**, touching every existing call site and test in `mc/boundary`. Compile-time discoveries, zero risk."
- ADR-021:84-88: "`Jurisdiction`'s zero value must be **safe, not empty**: ... a zero `Jurisdiction` passed to `Authorize` is a programming error that fails closed (rejects everything)".
The two claims are in the same document and do collide.

CODE BASELINE (mine): `grep -rn "\.Authorize("` in /mc yields exactly 13 hits, all in boundary/identity_test.go, ZERO production call sites. Baseline `mise exec -- go test ./boundary/` = ok.

PROBE (mine): wrote boundary/jurisdiction.go implementing D1's seam with an unexported `resolved bool` set only by ResolveJurisdiction; added the 4th param to Authorize (identity.go:231) and inserted `j.Rejects(identity)` at D6's stated position — after step 4/suffix, before ResolveAccess (ADR-021:198-200). Applied the mechanical migration via sed to all 13 sites passing `boundary.Jurisdiction{}`.

RESULT: `go build ./...` clean, `go vet` clean — so the "compile-time" half is true and nothing outside tests breaks (no production caller). But `go test ./boundary/` = 7 runtime failures across 3 test funcs, matching the reported list exactly: TestAuthorizeIdentityWalk/{descendant_accepted_with_derived_suffix, the_root_itself_has_an_empty_suffix, in-root_symlink_accepted, RW_over_an_RO_maximum_rejects (code = "mount.denied_root", want "mount.rw_not_permitted")}, TestAuthorizeBlockedMatching/a_workspace_merely_containing_a_blocked_name_is_fine, TestAuthorizeSuffixGrammar/{colon_in_the_allow_root_itself_is_legal, ordinary_descendant_of_a_colon_root_is_legal}.

REACHABILITY — I looked hard for an upstream rule that kills it, and found none. The obvious escape would be a cheap `ResolveJurisdiction(JurisdictionInput{})` in tests, but ADR-021's OWN required test at :354-355 ("no constructor path yields a `Jurisdiction` missing a non-operator member") plus D2's non-subtractability forbid a constructor that yields a usable value from empty input; D4's "Every comparison is `os.SameFile` on resolved objects" forces those members to be stat-able real directories. I probed a strict constructor: empty input errors, so there is no cheap path. I then probed the real fix — a shared `testJurisdiction(t)` helper materializing HOME + MC_HOME under t.TempDir() — and it restores green (suffix="project/src" access="ro"). So the fixture requirement is real.

SCOPE: in scope. The zero-value rule is ADR-021's own D1, and the falsified cost claim is ADR-021's own Consequences section — this re-litigates neither ADR-017 nor an operator decision. ADR-017 is silent on the seam's signature (gap (h)).

ONE CORRECTION TO THE FINDING: it slightly overstates cost — "each of the 13 sites needs a real on-disk HOME + MC_HOME fixture" is wrong; ONE shared helper serves all 13 (I proved this). Real cost is 1 fixture helper + 13 call-site edits. That does not rescue "zero risk".

SEVERITY: minor, not major. The design is implementable — the zero-value mechanism itself works (unexported `resolved` bars any outside package from minting a non-zero value, reproducing blocked.go:72-76's law without compiling members in). No invariant breaks, no escape, nothing unimplementable. What is falsified is a cost estimate in "What gets harder", not a load-bearing design rule. But it is not cosmetic either: per AGENTS.md §4 a session may not commit while red, and §8 names a mid-red quota death the worst handoff, so a session budgeting a sed-and-compile migration from this line lands mid-red. Real, bounded defect in the ADR's text.
verdicts.recommended_action: Fix ADR-021:383-384 before the slice is planned. Replace "Compile-time discoveries, zero risk." with an honest estimate naming the runtime consequence of D1's own zero-value rule, e.g.: "**`Authorize` grows a fourth parameter**, touching all 13 existing call sites in `mc/boundary` (no production caller yet). The compiler finds the sites, but D1's zero-value rule means the mechanical migration (passing `Jurisdiction{}`) turns 7 subtests across `TestAuthorizeIdentityWalk`, `TestAuthorizeBlockedMatching`, and `TestAuthorizeSuffixGrammar` red — a zero jurisdiction rejects every source by design. Budget a shared test fixture helper that materializes a real HOME + MC_HOME and calls `ResolveJurisdiction`; the 13 sites then thread it. Note that `RW_over_an_RO_maximum_rejects` changes code to `mount.denied_root` under a zero jurisdiction — that is D6's ordering working as specified, and the test needs a real jurisdiction to keep asserting `rw_not_permitted`." Also consider adding the fixture helper to the "Tests that pin it" list so the red-first TDD order accounts for it. No change to D1, D2, or D6 is warranted — the mechanism is sound and was verified working.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED, and if anything the finding understates its blast radius.

**The quotes check out (I re-read all of them, none were misattributed).**
- ADR-021:144-147 states the rule with no exception: "For canonical source `S` and protected root `P`, reject when `S == P`, `S` is a descendant of `P`, **or `S` is an ancestor of `P`**." D4:158-159 doubles down: "mounting the **parent** of another Worksource is explicitly required to reject" — the only case it considers.
- ADR-017:371-372 makes the nested roots union members in their own right: "every registered real Git control directory and every `<workspace_root>/.mission-control` task/projection root".
- ADR-017:718-720 creates them: "Onboarding creates the operator-owned non-symlink `.mission-control/{tasks,projections}` roots".
- ADR-017:300-302 requires the source to pass: "Every externally configured `workspace_root`, `artifact_roots`, and `readonly_mounts` source must match one allow entry." ADR-021 D9:296 itself names profile save as a call site.
- `grep -n "cover|workspace_root|own Worksource|task-local|nested" docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md` → only 209, 213, 384, all unrelated prose ("exists to cover"). ADR-021 never mentions covers, workspace_root, or the carve-out.

**PROBE (my worktree, `mc/boundary/probe_d4_internal_test.go`, internal test, mirror walk of the real `enclosesByIdentity` at identity.go:198-210, fixture = onboarding's exact layout under ADR-017 Decision 1's own `~/src/acme` example).** `mise exec -- go build ./...` clean; `go test ./boundary/` — only my probe is red, everything else green:
`ADR-021 D4 rejects source ".../src/acme": it is an ancestor of protected root ".../src/acme/.mission-control/tasks" -> mount.denied_root` (same for `projections` and `.git`).

**Reachability — genuinely reachable; nothing upstream makes it unconstructible.** I checked the three candidate escapes:
1. D3's whole-MC_HOME typed carve-out does not reach it — the workspace_root is externally configured (ADR-017:300-302), so it goes through `match()` as an ordinary/profile-requested mount, which ADR-017:396 says the union governs.
2. D7:252-257's identity-based own/other discrimination does not save it — that is scoped to the `cross_worksource` member (:368-369). D7:233-235 files Git-control dirs and `.mission-control` roots under `denied_root` with no own/other split.
3. The blocked floor is irrelevant (it would only add a rejection).

**Not a re-litigation of ADR-017 — ADR-017 has an answer and ADR-021 dropped it.** ADR-017 attaches the qualification directly to the member bullet, :372-374: "Decision 5 permits only the exact own task-local root, committed-tree materializations, trusted setup/landing, and **Homie's type-matched inert nested covers**". Decision 6 then makes the cover mandatory — :655: "registered live non-bare Worksource root **with every descendant Git control covered** … RO | Homie only", plus :656 (`.git` cover), :657 (each additional control descendant), :658 ("`.mission-control` | generated empty directory, RO cover over every MC task/projection root"). Under D4-as-written, ADR-017's entire Homie workspace-mount block is unbuildable — and ADR-017 wins on conflict (ADR-021:11). The extract ADR-021 is built on flags exactly this at extract:162-163 ("a member in their own right (:370-371), **with four permitted shapes (:371-373)**") and ADR-021 never carries it through. The spec agrees with the finding, not with D4 — §11.3:453 is downward-and-other-only: "no resolved mount may fall under **another** Worksource's roots or under a host credential directory."

**Understated:** the finding pins the break on onboarding, but `<workspace_root>/.git` is a "registered real Git control directory" (:371) too, so *every Git-backed workspace_root* is an ancestor of a protected root and rejects even before onboarding runs. My probe demonstrates both.

**Severity major:** unimplementable as specified. Profile save calls `Authorize(workspace_root, …)` → `mount.denied_root`, and ADR-021 D9:298 / ADR-017:1165 make that abort the whole plan ("Every code aborts the whole plan; none drops one mount"). No Git-backed or onboarded Worksource can be saved or mounted; Homie's workspace mount can never plan. ADR-021's own test list (:345-379) never constructs a workspace_root containing its own nested protected roots, so the design ships green.
verdicts.recommended_action: Do not TDD ADR-021 as written. D4 must carry ADR-017:372-374's carve-out clause explicitly: the ancestor direction rejects for the `denied_paths`, MC_HOME, sessions, runtime-control, gateway/CA, HOME-class, and **other**-Worksource members, but the nested `<workspace_root>/.mission-control` and registered-Git-control members are answered by ADR-017 Decision 6's covers (:655-658), not by rejecting their own containing workspace_root. Concretely: (1) add a D4 exception naming the nested-cover mechanism, scoped by resolved identity to the own Worksource (D7:252-257's predicate already exists — extend it from `cross_worksource` to these two members); (2) fix D7:233-235, which currently files Git-control dirs and `.mission-control` roots under `denied_root` unqualified; (3) state that the cover set is a planner obligation ADR-021 does not own (the D-mount-plan slice does), so the seam is explicit rather than silently absent; (4) add the missing fixture to the test list at :358-361 — an onboarded, Git-backed workspace_root, allowlisted rw, must **authorize**, and the same root must still reject when it is *another* Worksource's. Also add it as a planted mutant (:376-379): "ancestor rule applied to the own Worksource's nested roots" must die with an exercised witness. My probe is preserved at `/Users/vinchenkov/Documents/dev/ai/homie/.claude/worktrees/wf_48d38522-ea8-10/mc/boundary/probe_d4_internal_test.go` as a red-first starting point.

```
A. os.SameFile(live, synth-with-Stat_t) = false
B. os.SameFile(live, synth-no-Sys)      = false
C. os.SameFile(live, live2) [control]   = true
ESCAPE: ".../sessions/run-0123456789abcdef" is a descendant of protected root
".../sessions", injected (dev,ino) is CORRECT, yet D4's os.SameFile walk never
matched -> Rejects returns nil -> FAILS OPEN
control: live-FileInfo walk rejects = true
```
Leg A is the decisive one: a *correctly populated* `*syscall.Stat_t` behind `Sys()` still yields `false`. The composition fails open, not closed. Probe removed; `go build ./...` and `go test ./boundary/` green; worktree clean.

**Reachability — I actively tried to kill this and could not.** The obvious upstream saves don't apply. `mc/boundary/blocked.go:28-69` has no `sessions` and no `MC_HOME` pattern, so step 3 (`blocked.Rejects`) does not catch `MC_HOME/sessions/run-<other>`; jurisdiction is the only gate. D6 (`:209-217`) narrows observability to allowlisted paths — the scenario allowlists `MC_HOME`, which D6 itself names as "exactly the case :393-394 exists to cover". So the state is constructible and the escape is real.

**Not re-litigation.** ADR-017 never defines `JurisdictionInput`; the seam is gap (g)/(h) (extract:240-248), i.e. precisely what ADR-021 exists to resolve. The contradiction is *internal to ADR-021* — D1 versus D4 — so it is squarely in scope.

**One thing the finding missed that strengthens it and names the fix.** ADR-017:244 reads: "stat the resolved object, and retain `(device,inode,type)` **with its canonical path**." ADR-017's identity evidence is the triple *plus the canonical path*. D1 kept the triple and dropped the canonical-path half of ADR-017's own sentence. Under the stated "ADR-017 wins" rule, D1's triple-only payload is a deviation from ADR-017, and ADR-017's full sentence already supplies the remedy. The extract seeded the truncation at :117 ("pre-resolved to `(device,inode,type)`"), but its purity constraint at :120 forbids querying "the spine or the filesystem registry" — **not the filesystem**. `mc/boundary` stats freely today (identity.go:140, :204, :289), so carrying canonical paths and stat-ing them at construction violates no purity rule. D1's own reason 3 therefore does not justify dropping the path.

Also confirming the finding's secondary claim: D1's `BlockPolicy` model does not transfer — `BlockPolicy` compares **strings** (blocked.go:72-76) and never round-trips a filesystem identity through a struct. And the "Tests that pin it" list (`:347-379`) would not catch this: every entry constructs protected roots from real paths, and the five planted mutants (`:376-379`) do not include triple-synthesis. A test helper that stats the path passes while production injection fails open.

**Severity: major** on two independent grounds — the design is *unimplementable as literally specified* (D1 and D4 are mutually unsatisfiable), and the naive literal resolution *silently breaks the fail-closed posture*, authorizing every protected root with no error.
verdicts.recommended_action:
Amend D1 before implementation to restore ADR-017:244's full sentence: `JurisdictionInput` should carry each protected root's **canonical path together with its `(device,inode,type)` triple**, not the triple alone. `ResolveJurisdiction` then `os.Stat`s each canonical path once at construction, storing the live `fs.FileInfo` on the `Jurisdiction` value — exactly the `resolvedRoot{canonical, info}` shape already used at identity.go:156-160. This keeps D4's "every comparison is `os.SameFile`" literally true and satisfiable, keeps the anti-prefix guarantee intact, and requires no change to D4's text. The triple stays as TOCTOU evidence: if the stat at construction disagrees with the caller-supplied triple, `ResolveJurisdiction` must reject (fail-closed) — this also serves D9's recheck. Reject the alternative of dropping D4's `os.SameFile` in favour of a hand-rolled dev/ino predicate: it is a larger deviation and re-opens ADR-017:1348-1349's rejected-alternative reasoning for no benefit.

Note in D1 that this does not breach the purity rule — phase3-contract:41-44 and extract:120 forbid querying the spine and the Git registry, not the filesystem, which `mc/boundary` already stats at identity.go:140, :204, :289. Resolution of *which paths are protected* stays host-side; only the stat is local.

Add to "Tests that pin it" a test that exercises the **injection path as production will build it** (construct `JurisdictionInput` from a triple/path pair, not from a test-only helper that stats a live path), plus a sixth planted mutant: "protected-root FileInfo synthesized rather than stat-ed — `os.SameFile` returns false and jurisdiction fails open." Without that mutant the whole existing test list passes over this defect.

Finally, resolve D8 against the amended D1: the nearest-existing-ancestor rule already presumes paths, which is corroborating evidence that D1's triple-only payload was an oversight rather than a considered choice.

HOME is injected, not ambient" — entirely about `JurisdictionInput` carrying HOME. CONFIRMED — I re-derived every element myself rather than trusting the finding's quotes.

**1. The "D5" carve-out does not exist.** `grep -n "D5" docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md` returns exactly three hits: `:137`, `:179`, `:328`. It is never read from `$HOME` inside `mc/boundary`." It contains no per-class check. So both pointers are dangling: `:136-138` "the typed-grant carve-out (D5): a typed source must be checked against the exact class it claims" and `:327-329` "it must be added as an explicit typed carve-out (D5's per-class check)". I also ran `grep -n -i "carve\|typed\|class\|grant"` over the whole ADR: every "carve-out" hit is one of those two dangling references. No decision in ADR-021 defines the mechanism.

The signature admits no class.** ADR-021:58 fixes `func (j Jurisdiction) Rejects(id SourceIdentity) error`, and `mc/boundary/identity.go:110-116` is verbatim `SourceIdentity{Raw, RawClean, Canonical string; Info fs.FileInfo; IsDir bool}` — no class, no owning run/session. This is a closed exception, not a way for an allowlist entry to override `MC_HOME` protection."

**3. Probe — both failure arms are reachable, not merely arguable.** I built `mc/boundary/probe021/probe_test.go` implementing D1's exact signature, D3's whole-tree `MC_HOME` root, and D4's `os.SameFile`-based bidirectional rule (mirroring the real `enclosesByIdentity`, identity.go:198-210), then drove ADR-017 Decision 6's real grants through it. `mise exec -- go test ./boundary/probe021/ -v` passes and logs:

- Arm (b): `Rejects(own-session)`, `Rejects(other-run-session)`, and `Rejects(MC_HOME/mount-allowlist)` return *identical* verdicts. The predicate's only inputs are `j` and `SourceIdentity`, so any bypass admitting the own grant necessarily admits the sibling and the allowlist — precisely what ADR-017:400-401 forbids.

Not re-litigating ADR-017, and not out of scope.** ADR-017 *mandates* the carve-out (:381) and the per-class confinement (:396-401); ADR-021 does not dispute either — it asserts a mechanism it never defines. verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. I re-read every primary source and verified all quotes verbatim, then drove the real path rather than reasoning about it.

**Quotes verified (my own `sed`/`grep`, not the finding's):**
- `docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md:283-296` — D9's header is "**Implements ADR-017:264-269 and :1339-1341.**" and its operative sentence is: "That forbids memoizing a jurisdiction verdict keyed on source identity … `Rejects` is a pure function of `(identity, jurisdiction)` and is re-evaluated at every call site: profile save (:237-238, spec:453), plan, pre-create, and post-create/pre-start." The re-evaluation obligation lands on `Rejects` only; `jurisdiction` is a caller-supplied parameter and D9 imposes nothing on its freshness.
- `docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md:335-340` (Sharp edge 4) — "Building it is host-side work with real `os.Stat` cost at **every spawn**, and D9 forbids caching it away. … the fix is caching keyed on the *jurisdiction's* own generation". "Every spawn" is once per spawn; step 7 needs plan + pre-create + pre-start. I confirmed ADR-021 defines no "generation" mechanism anywhere (`grep -n "generation"` returns only :340).
- `docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md:264-269` — "rerun **the whole predicate**: … protected/cross-Worksource roots; kind, access, and final destination. Any drift removes the unstarted container."
- `docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md:1339-1341` — "Permission/ACL, blocked-policy, allow-root, and protected-set changes with unchanged source bytes/inode also remove the unstarted container." This sentence is only satisfiable by re-observing the protected set: a protected-set change with an unchanged source inode is undetectable from the source alone.

**Scope check (the finding's biggest risk — it survives).** This is not re-litigating ADR-017: ADR-017 plainly requires the re-run, and the question is only whether ADR-021 carries it into the seam it defines. Nor is it out-of-scope host-side work: `grep -n "step 7\|recheck\|rerun"` shows D9:287 is the *only* place ADR-021 addresses step 7, and D9 does reach into caller obligations — it enumerates four call sites for `Rejects`. Having chosen to speak on the recheck cadence, it specifies half of it.

**Probe (my worktree, `mc/boundary/probe_d9_internal_test.go`).** I modeled exactly what D9 mandates — `os.SameFile` on resolved objects (D4:145-147) with a snapshot `FileInfo`, mirroring how `identity.go:180` freezes `resolvedRoot{… info: identity.Info}` at construction and `enclosesByIdentity` (`identity.go:198-210`) compares a fresh `os.Stat` against that stored `ancestor.info`. Scenario: another Worksource's registered root is reached via a symlink (ordinary re-registration/re-clone repoints one); it resolves to inode N at plan; the candidate allowlisted source is inode M and is authorized. The symlink is then repointed at the candidate's directory. `mise exec -- go test ./boundary/ -run TestProbeD9 -v` output:

    plan: authorized (source inode M != protected root inode N)
    recheck: source bytes/inode unchanged, as ADR-017:1339-1341 stipulates
    D9 as written (re-run Rejects, jurisdiction NOT re-resolved): NO REJECT -> container starts with another Worksource's workspace root mounted
    re-resolved jurisdiction: REJECT -> ADR-017:264-269 satisfied

This closes the reachability question the finding's own scenario left shaky. I first tried the finding's literal rmdir+recreate/rename-swap framing and it **self-defeats**: if the candidate path is the protected path, it rejects at plan; and a rename swap makes the candidate's own canonical path vanish, so step 7's re-resolution of the source fails with ENOENT and rejects anyway. The symlink-repoint variant is the one that constructs the state — the source's canonical path and inode are genuinely unchanged (asserted in the probe via `os.SameFile(sourceAtPlan, sourceAtRecheck)`), which is precisely :1339-1341's stated premise. So the conclusion holds even though the offered scenario was imprecise.

**Why major.** D9's header claim to implement :264-269 and :1339-1341 is load-bearing and is falsified by D9's own purity argument: re-running a function that is pure in `(identity, jurisdiction)` against an unchanged `jurisdiction` necessarily returns the plan-time answer. The natural implementation of ADR-021 as written — Sharp edge 4 prices the build once "at every spawn", then passes that value to plan/pre-create/pre-start — satisfies D9 literally while violating ADR-017 and permitting another Worksource's workspace root into a started container. The "generation"-keyed cache blessing is a secondary aggravator (a hedge, not a decision), but with no on-disk observation tied to it, it is a stale-jurisdiction cache by construction.

Mitigating but not disqualifying: ADR-017 wins on conflict and independently binds the host, so an implementer reading both may re-resolve anyway; nothing is built yet; and the fix is one sentence. That is an argument for cheapness, not for correctness — and catching it pre-build is exactly what this design review is for.
verdicts.recommended_action: Amend D9 before TDD begins. Add the missing obligation explicitly — something like: "`ResolveJurisdiction` itself re-runs at profile save, plan, pre-create, and post-create/pre-start; a changed protected-root identity is itself drift under :264-269. Re-running `Rejects` against a plan-time `Jurisdiction` value does not satisfy :1339-1341." Correspondingly fix Sharp edge 4's "at every spawn" to price the build at every predicate evaluation (three per spawn, plus profile save), and either delete the "generation"-keyed cache remark or bind the generation to a filesystem observation of the protected roots — as written it is a stale-identity cache with extra steps. Finally, strengthen the "No caching (D9)" test line under "Tests that pin it" (:374-375): "the same source identity with a changed jurisdiction flips the verdict" only exercises the pure function and would pass against the defective design. It needs a sibling test that replaces a protected root on disk between plan and recheck with the source inode held constant, and asserts the recheck rejects — plus a planted mutant ("jurisdiction resolved once at plan and reused") that must die. The probe at `/Users/vinchenkov/Documents/dev/ai/homie/.claude/worktrees/wf_48d38522-ea8-15/mc/boundary/probe_d9_internal_test.go` is a working starting point for that test; note the symlink-repoint construction, since the rmdir+recreate framing does not reach the state.

verdicts.reasoning:
I re-read all primary sources and ran my own probe. The finding is a conjunction; its two halves came apart under test.

WHAT I CONFIRMED (the smaller half). D5's body is exactly ADR-021:179-192, titled "HOME is injected, not ambient", and by direct read it contains no class, kind, or carve-out concept — it is entirely about injecting HOME rather than reading `$HOME`. `grep -n "carve\|class\|kind\|typed\|D5"` over ADR-021 returns only two pointers to a per-class mechanism — :137 "the typed-grant carve-out (D5): a typed source must be checked against the exact class it claims" and :328 "an explicit typed carve-out (D5's per-class check)" — and no definition anywhere. ADR-021's numbering convention is unambiguous (ADR-017's is spelled "Decision 5" at :7/:21; its own are "D1".."D9", cf. ":110 D6's ordering"), so both are genuinely dangling. The test list :370-371 does pin only the deny half. All true.

WHAT I REFUTED (the load-bearing half, which carries the "major"). The claim is that "own-session and sibling-session present identically and must receive identical verdicts" and that "the signature admits no third option: ... only a class/kind parameter Rejects does not have can satisfy both halves". This is false. `SourceIdentity` (identity.go:110-116) carries `Canonical string` and `Info fs.FileInfo` — the exact `os.SameFile` handle. Own and sibling sessions are distinct inodes, so they do NOT present identically. I built D1's exact signature `Rejects(id SourceIdentity) error` over D3's whole-tree MC_HOME root, plus an identity-keyed carve-out set carried on the Jurisdiction value, compiled it and ran it in my worktree:

  Rejects(own session (ADR-017:396-399 REQUIRES permit)) -> <nil>
  Rejects(sibling session (ADR-017:399 requires deny)) -> ... "is at or under protected MC_HOME"
  Rejects(sessions/ ancestor (deny)) -> ... "is at or under protected MC_HOME"

Both halves of :396-401 are satisfied by the signature as written. The other reviewer's probe showed identical verdicts only because their probe had no carve-out set — they probed a strawman, not the signature's expressive limit. The mechanism is not even novel: ADR-021 D7:252-257 already specifies precisely this for own/other Worksources — "Own/other discrimination is identity-based, never name-based ... JurisdictionInput therefore carries the own Worksource's identity explicitly; a root matching it is not a cross-Worksource rejection."

The residual kind-discrimination gap is out of ADR-021's scope. ADR-017:349-351 enumerates five DISTINCT checks a typed source owes — "kind-specific source-type, non-symlink, identity, containment, and cross-Worksource". Confinement to its one kind-specific root is the *containment* check, not jurisdiction; ADR-021 defines only the jurisdiction seam.

FAILURE SCENARIO NOT REACHABLE. It requires that no carve-out be expressible — refuted above. It is also independently guarded: ADR-021:385-388 states the host side owes JurisdictionInput and "Until then `Authorize` still must not be wired into production planning", so no spawn path calls this in this slice.

NOT RE-LITIGATION: the carve-out need is ADR-017's own (:375 protects MC_HOME/sessions; :396-401 carves out the own session), so D3 widens but does not create it. The surviving defect is ADR-021's own text.

Also checked: the nine gaps in the extract §8 are (a)-(i) and do NOT include the typed carve-out mechanism, so ADR-021:7's scope claim never undertook to specify it. What survives is that ADR-021 twice promises a mechanism it does not define and points at the wrong decision — a real but textual, bounded defect, not an unimplementable design.
verdicts.recommended_action: Do not treat as a blocker. Fix as a documentation defect before TDD: (1) delete or correct the two dangling "D5" pointers at ADR-021:137 and :328 — the carve-out belongs to D3 (which creates the whole-tree rule) or to ADR-017:396-401's containment check, not to D5/HOME; (2) state D3's carve-out mechanism in one sentence, reusing D7:252-257's existing law verbatim — JurisdictionInput carries the exact typed own-source grants as resolved identities, and a source matching one by os.SameFile is not a denied_root rejection; (3) add the missing permit-half test beside :370-371: "Rejects permits the exact own session inside MC_HOME while denying its sibling (:396-401)" — currently only the deny half is pinned, which is the one genuine risk here; (4) note explicitly that kind-specific confinement (ADR-017:397) is the separate containment check of :350, out of this slice's scope, so a future reader does not expect Rejects to own it.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a real, demonstrable gap — with one arm stronger than the finding states and one arm weaker.

**The quotes are accurate.** I re-read all of them in my worktree. `docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md:183-184` reads verbatim "carries the operator's real HOME as an **explicit, already-resolved** value. It is never read from `$HOME` inside `mc/boundary`." D1's law at :84-87 is scoped exactly as quoted — "a **zero** `Jurisdiction` passed to `Authorize` is a programming error that fails closed". D4:161-165 and D8:272-274 are as quoted.

**The absence is real, not an oversight of the reviewer's reading.** I grepped the whole ADR: `grep -nE "valid|trust|absolut|ownerUID|non-root|exist"` returns "validated" only in D2's *title* (:91) — the body of D2 specifies only the 512-`denied_paths` bound and non-subtractability. No absoluteness, non-root, existence, kind, or ownership check appears anywhere in the document. Decisively, D2's own member enumeration (:98-100) is "(`denied_paths`) and the MC-derived ones (MC_HOME roots, HOME-class roots, gateway/CA, sessions, other Worksources' roots, Git control dirs)" — **HOME is not on that list at all**. So D2's "cannot be constructed without its non-operator members" does not even reach HOME; the gap is wider than the finding claims. The test list (:349-375) tests that the `broad_root` rule is *computed correctly* from a non-`/Users` HOME, but never that a bogus HOME is rejected.

**Not a re-litigation.** The extract's gap (e) (`docs/reviews/2026-07-14-adr-017-jurisdiction-extract.md:229-232`) is "How is 'the operator's real HOME' obtained? ... env `HOME` is caller-influenceable." D5 exists to resolve gap (e). It answered *where the value comes from* and not *whether the arriving value is sound*. That is squarely inside D5's mandate, not ADR-017's. Nothing upstream makes the state unconstructible: ADR-017 is silent on HOME's provenance (that silence *is* gap (e)).

**Probe (my worktree, `mise exec -- go test ./boundary/ -run TestProbeFloorOnHomeScenarioPaths`), zero-value `BlockPolicy`:**
```
floor rejects "/Users/op"                   = false
floor rejects "/Users/op/Library"           = false
floor rejects "/Users"                      = false
floor rejects "/"                           = false
floor rejects "/Library/Keychains"          = true
floor rejects "/Users/op/Library/Keychains" = true
floor rejects "/Users/op/.ssh"              = true
```
The floor confirms the finding's premise: `users`, `op`, `library` are no patterns (`mc/boundary/blocked.go:28-69`).

**The strongest arm is one the finding under-sells.** With `HOME="/"`, D4's *own flagship worked example* — the one it calls "**load-bearing, not redundant with the blocked floor**" and makes "also a required test" (:152-159) — silently dies. `~/Library` rejects today only because it is an ancestor of the present HOME-class root `~/Library/Keychains`. Re-anchored, that member becomes `/Library/Keychains`, which I verified **does exist** on this machine (`drwxr-xr-x 9 root wheel /Library/Keychains`) — so it is "present" and stays a member, but it is the *wrong* one: `/Users/op/Library` is not an ancestor of it, so bidirectionality does not fire. The floor does not match the address `/Users/op/Library` (probe above), and ADR-017:229-231 is explicit that the floor "classif[ies] the requested mount **address**, not the recursive contents". An `[[allow]]` entry for `/Users/op/Library` therefore authorizes a mount containing the operator's real keychain. This arm has **no** backstop and does not depend on registry state.

**The finding's arm (1) is weaker than claimed.** "An `[[allow]]` entry for `/Users/op` then authorizes the operator's entire real HOME" is contingent, not certain. D4's ancestor direction still fires if any protected root sits under `/Users/op` — and ADR-017:370-373 makes every registered Git control dir and every `<workspace_root>/.mission-control` root a member (the carve-outs at :371-373 do not exempt the real Git control dir), while D8 keeps even absent ones as members. Since §5's model puts workspaces under HOME, a registered Git-backed Worksource ordinarily rescues this case with `mount.denied_root`. It fails only with zero such roots under HOME. I would not report arm (1) as unconditional.

**Severity: minor, matching the claim.** It is a genuine, demonstrable, bounded specification gap, but the trigger is a defect in host-side code that ADR-021 explicitly does not write ("The host side owes `JurisdictionInput`", :385-388) — it is not reachable from operator config alone. It also is not unique to HOME in kind: an incomplete injected other-Worksource root list fails open identically, so "validate the injection" cannot be an absolute demand. What makes HOME different, and what makes this worth fixing rather than waving through, is that (a) HOME is cheaply and locally checkable inside the pure package, (b) D5 spends its entire rationale arguing HOME's trustworthiness and then closes with "A boundary that can be relocated by an environment variable is not a boundary" (:188-189) while leaving the relocation available through the struct field, and (c) D1 shows the authors had malformed inputs in scope and simply drew the line one notch too narrow. Not major: no invariant breaks under correct inputs, and the design is pre-TDD, which is exactly when this should land.

**One correction to the finding's remedy.** It proposes "the same seam `TrustHomeDir` already applies to MC_HOME at `identity.go:68-77`". Applied verbatim that is wrong: `trustedOwnerMode` rejects on `perm&0o077 != 0` (`identity.go:93`), and a real macOS HOME is not 0700 — mine is `drwxr-x---` (0750), commonly 0755. `TrustHomeDir` would reject every genuine operator HOME. The mode leg must be dropped for this member.
verdicts.recommended_action:
Fix before TDD; small, local edits to ADR-021 — no ADR-017 or spec change needed.

1. **D2**: add HOME to the enumerated members at :98-100 and state its validation explicitly. `ResolveJurisdiction` must reject a HOME that is not absolute, not lexically clean/symlink-resolved, equal to `/`, non-existent, not a directory, or not owned by `ownerUID`. Reuse `trustedLstat` + the uid leg of `trustedOwnerMode` (`identity.go:81-104`) — but **not** `TrustHomeDir` verbatim: its `perm&0o077 != 0` check (`identity.go:93`) rejects a real macOS HOME (mine is 0750; 0755 is common). Ownership by `ownerUID` is the load-bearing leg here, not the mode bits.

2. **D1**: widen the fail-closed law from "a *zero* `Jurisdiction`" (:84-87) to "any `Jurisdiction` not returned by a successful `ResolveJurisdiction`". As written, the law leaves populated-but-wrong values uncovered, which is exactly the hole.

3. **D5**: state that injection *moves* the trust anchor and that `ResolveJurisdiction` is where it is re-established — otherwise :188-189's standard indicts D5's own design.

4. **Tests** (:349-375): add to the `broad_root` list — a HOME of `""`, `/`, a relative path, a non-existent path, a regular file, or one not owned by `ownerUID` each rejects at construction. Add a planted mutant to the :376-379 list: *HOME validation removed* must die with an exercised witness that `/Users/op/Library` is authorized — that is D4's own required `~/Library` case, and it is the arm with no backstop.

Do not restate arm (1) ("authorizes the entire real HOME") as unconditional: D4's ancestor direction over the registered Git-control / `.mission-control` members (ADR-017:370-373) ordinarily catches `/Users/op`. Lead with the `~/Library`/Keychains arm — it is unconditional and it kills D4's own flagship example.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED THE QUOTES MYSELF — all are accurate. `docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md:265-271` reads verbatim: "You cannot `os.SameFile` a path with no inode, so this is an algorithm requirement, not a footnote." followed by "resolve the nearest existing canonical ancestor, retain the unresolved suffix, and compare on that pair. **Ambiguity denies** — if it cannot be decided whether `S` intersects the would-be path, reject." That is genuinely the entire specification of the comparison. D4:146-147 reads "Every comparison is `os.SameFile` on resolved objects — never a string prefix". ADR-017:273 and :1348-1349 are quoted correctly.

PROBES (mc/boundary/probe_d8_test.go in my worktree, `mise exec -- go test ./boundary/ -run TestProbe -v`, all passing):

1. Divergence is real. Deny `~/ws/secret` absent, `~/ws/secret-evil` exists. D8's own algorithm yields ancestor=`.../ws`, suffix=`secret`; source suffix below that same ancestor (derived by an `os.SameFile` identity walk, the shape `match()` uses at identity.go:284-303) = `secret-evil`. Output: "byte-prefix says intersects=true ; component-wise says intersects=false". So the two readings of "compare on that pair" disagree, and the byte reading reproduces exactly ADR-017:273's `/safe-root-evil` never satisfies `/safe-root` bug — in the one half D4's identity rule structurally cannot reach.

2. The escape direction really is unconstructible, as the reviewer conceded. My probe confirms this volume is case-insensitive ("stat ws/Secret when ws/secret exists: err=<nil>"), which closes the case-variant loophole rather than opening it: if any case variant of the deny path existed, `os.Stat` on the deny spelling would SUCCEED and it would be a present member, not an absent one. And a canonical source cannot sit under an absent path — every component of it would have to exist. My symlink attempt (probe 3) only produced a deny path that RESOLVES, i.e. the present case. So the defect is fail-closed: over-blocking (false rejection), never escape.

SCOPE — one correction to the finding. Its title is misframed. D8 does not "reintroduce the lexical comparison ADR-017 rejected"; ADR-017:275-276 itself MANDATES nearest-existing-ancestor-plus-unresolved-suffix. Attacking that mechanism choice would be re-litigating ADR-017 and out of scope. What is squarely in scope and does survive: ADR-021 declares this "an algorithm requirement, not a footnote" and then restates the footnote. D8's header claims "implements ADR-017:275-276", and the extract's §7 item 5 (:177-180) flags it as "A real algorithm requirement". It is not implemented. The Tests-that-pin-it line at :372-373 ("a non-existent deny path resolves through its nearest existing ancestor; ambiguity denies") cannot be written red-first against an undefined predicate. Secondarily, D4's absolute "never a string prefix" is the wording that overreaches — ADR-017 carves out this case, so the ADR set is coherent, but ADR-021 does not notice D8 is the exception to its own D4.

SEVERITY — I judge minor independently, agreeing with the reviewer. No invariant breaks; fail-closed posture is preserved (it errs toward denial); no escape (probe 2); implementable, just underconstrained. Not major.
verdicts.recommended_action:
Fix D8 before TDD (docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md:268-271). Three additions, all textual:

1. Say how the pair is compared. The ancestor half is `os.SameFile` on resolved objects; the suffix half is compared COMPONENT-WISE against the suffix derived from the same identity walk that located the ancestor — never as a byte prefix. Intersection holds when either component list is a component-prefix of the other (source under the would-be path, or source an ancestor of it). My probe shows this is the difference between rejecting and accepting `~/ws/secret-evil` against absent deny `~/ws/secret`.

2. State the case rule. Note that an absent deny path implies no case variant exists on a case-insensitive volume (my probe confirms `os.Stat` sees case variants), so component comparison may be exact-byte here without guessing the volume's case mode — which is what ADR-017:274 asks for ("without guessing its case mode").

3. Enumerate "ambiguity". At minimum: the nearest-existing-ancestor resolution itself traversed a symlink whose identity is unstable, or the ancestor is not on the source's identity chain in either direction while the suffixes cannot be ordered. Without an enumeration the :372-373 test has no predicate.

Also add one clause to D4 (:146-147) acknowledging D8 as the sanctioned exception to "never a string prefix", pointing at ADR-017:275-276 as the authority for it — so a future reader does not read D4 as forbidding what ADR-017 mandates.

Note for the parent: the finding's TITLE should be re-worded when logged. The mechanism is ADR-017's own decision, not something D8 reintroduces against it; the defect is underspecification of a mechanism ADR-017 requires.

verdicts.reasoning:
I verified each element independently against the files in my own worktree, and reproduced the probe myself.

**1. The signature is identity-only — confirmed.** ADR-021:57-58 reads verbatim: `// Callable WITHOUT an allow-root match — typed system sources need exactly this.` / `func (j Jurisdiction) Rejects(id SourceIdentity) error`. I then read the real `SourceIdentity` at `mc/boundary/identity.go:110-118`: it has exactly `Raw, RawClean, Canonical string; Info fs.FileInfo; IsDir bool`. Nothing in it names a kind, class, or claimed grant. `ResolveSource` (identity.go:121-152) populates only those fields. So the predicate structurally cannot know which typed grant a caller claims.

**2. D3 makes MC_HOME whole — confirmed.** ADR-021:121: "**the whole `MC_HOME` tree is one protected root**".

**3. The "D5 per-class check" does not exist — confirmed by exhaustive grep, not by reading.** `grep -n "carve-out|per-class|typed|Typed" docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md` returns 9 hits; the only two that name a mechanism both point at D5 — :137 ("the typed-grant carve-out (D5): a typed source must be checked against the exact class it claims") and :328 ("an explicit typed carve-out (D5's per-class check)"). I read D5 in full (:179-192): heading "### D5. HOME is injected, not ambient", body entirely `$HOME` vs `tool_home_dir` vs `ownerUID`. No per-class check, no carve-out, no typed source. No other decision in ADR-021 defines one. `grep` for "own session|own state|projection|task-local" in ADR-021 returns **zero** hits for the first three — the four grant classes ADR-017:397-399 names are never mentioned in ADR-021 at all.

**4. ADR-017 requires the permit half — confirmed.** :376-380: "all `MC_HOME` attachment, output, workflow, ... and runtime-auth roots, including the allowlist, **except each exact typed own-source grant in Decisions 4, 6, and 8**". :396-401: "the exact own session, derived own state, selected runtime-control directory, or generated projection may be inside `MC_HOME`, but any sibling/ancestor/other identity is still denied."

**5. Probe — I wrote and ran it myself** (`mc/boundary/zz_probe_test.go`, D1's exact signature + D3's whole-tree root + D4's bidirectional `os.SameFile` walk, `mise exec -- go test ./boundary/ -run TestProbe_TypedOwnSession -v`, PASS):
```
Rejects(sess-own)   = source ".../mc-home/sessions/sess-own" is under protected root MC_HOME(whole)
Rejects(sess-other) = source ".../mc-home/sessions/sess-other" is under protected root MC_HOME(whole)
```
Identical verdicts. The own typed grant of ADR-017:663 (`/mc/session` ← `MC_HOME/sessions/<run-or-session-id>/`, RW) is rejected, and the predicate has no input by which it could differ.

**6. The test list would not catch it — confirmed.** ADR-021:370-371 is the only typed test: "`Rejects` is callable with no allowlist and still denies a sibling/ancestor/other identity inside `MC_HOME` (:396-401)". It cites :396-401 — the sentence with both a permit and a deny half — but asserts only the deny half, which my probe passes. There is no permit-half test.

**7. D7 does not rescue it — confirmed.** ADR-021:252-257 injects "the own Worksource's identity explicitly", scoped to `mount.cross_worksource` (the :368-369 member per :230-231). It says nothing about own vs other typed MC_HOME grants.

**8. The `.mission-control` sub-claim — confirmed.** ADR-017:371-373 permits "only the exact own task-local root, committed-tree materializations, trusted setup/landing, and Homie's type-matched inert nested covers". ADR-021 mentions `.mission-control` exactly twice (:233 in D7's flat `denied_root` list, :350 in the union-membership test), never the four shapes.

**Reachability.** I tested the strongest counterargument: ADR-017:396-397 says "That union governs ordinary/profile-requested mounts. A typed system mount is **instead** confined to its one kind-specific authorized root" — so perhaps a correct implementer never calls `Rejects` on a typed source, making the spawn breakage unconstructible. That defense fails on ADR-021's own text: D1 rationale #1 (:67-73) insists typed sources "still owe the check" and that this is why `Rejects` is "exported and total on its own"; :312-313 claims as a benefit that "Jurisdiction is reachable by typed system sources (D1)"; and test :370-371 designates `Rejects` as the enforcer of :396-401. Under that reading the own session rejects (my probe). Under the defensive reading, `Rejects` still rejects the own session and the "kind-specific check" is supposed to permit it — but ADR-021 defines no such check anywhere. Both readings land on the same hole: the mechanism ADR-017 mandates is absent, and ADR-021 twice cites a decision that does not define it. Not unconstructible; not shadowed by the blocked floor (step 3 has no `sessions` pattern — `blocked.go:28-69`); not shadowed by step-4 ordering, since D6:215-216 explicitly routes typed sources around it ("or for a typed source calling `Rejects` directly").

**Scope.** Not re-litigation. ADR-017:376-380's "except each exact typed own-source grant" is ADR-017's own reservation; the finding judges ADR-021 solely on whether it resolves it. It does not.

**Severity.** No invariant break and no escape — the failure is fail-closed. But it is major on the "unimplementable as specified / falsifies a load-bearing claim" limbs: an implementer cannot build :396-401's permit half from ADR-021; D3:136-138's stated justification for retaining the fifteen-class enumeration rests entirely on a decision that does not exist, voiding D3's rationale as written; and :312-313's benefit claim is false for the permit half. For a design under pre-implementation review, the gap sits precisely where the ADR claims to have closed it.
verdicts.recommended_action:
ADR-021 must gain a real decision defining the typed-grant carve-out before TDD, since D3 and Sharp-edge 2 already promise one and D1's signature cannot express it. Concretely:

1. **Give the predicate the discriminator.** `Rejects(id SourceIdentity) error` cannot carry a grant claim — `SourceIdentity` (identity.go:110-118) is pure filesystem identity. Either widen it (`Rejects(id SourceIdentity, claim GrantClaim) error`, where the zero `GrantClaim` means "ordinary/profile-requested" and fails closed against the whole union) or add a second entry point (`RejectsTyped(id SourceIdentity, class TypedClass, authorized ResolvedRoot) error`). Prefer the form that keeps the ordinary path's fail-closed default reachable by the zero value, matching D1's own zero-value law (:84-89).

2. **Enumerate the permitted classes, closed.** ADR-017:397-399 names four — exact own session, derived own state, selected runtime-control directory, generated projection — carved out of :376-380's fifteen MC_HOME classes. `JurisdictionInput` must carry the caller's *own* authorized root identity per class (mirroring D7's own-Worksource injection at :252-257), so the check is "does `S` equal the exact authorized root for the class it claims" by `os.SameFile`, with sibling/ancestor/other identity still denied per :399-401.

3. **Restore ADR-017:371-373's four `.mission-control` shapes.** D7:233-235 currently lists `.mission-control` roots as a flat `denied_root` member; the exact own task-local root, committed-tree materializations, trusted setup/landing, and Homie's type-matched inert nested covers must appear as carve-outs or a Worker's own `/workspace` bind (ADR-017:636) rejects.

4. **Fix the two dangling references** (:137, :328) to point at the new decision.

5. **Add the permit-half tests.** The current typed test (:370-371) asserts only denial and passes on a prototype that rejects everything. Pair it: own session at `MC_HOME/sessions/<id>` **accepts** while its sibling rejects; own task-local root accepts while another task's rejects; selected runtime-control dir accepts while other runtime-control dirs reject (this is ADR-017:1174's "selected vs other runtime-control dirs" acceptance row, currently unsatisfiable). Add a planted mutant: "carve-out widened from exact-identity to inside-MC_HOME" must die.

If the operator prefers to keep this slice narrow, the alternative is to scope ADR-021 explicitly to ordinary/profile-requested mounts (ADR-017:396-397's "That union governs ordinary/profile-requested mounts"), delete D1 rationale #1, :312-313, and test :370-371's typed claims, and park the typed carve-out for a follow-on ADR — but then `Authorize` still may not be wired into the typed spawn path, and that must be stated as loudly as :385-388 states the JurisdictionInput debt.

verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED — I re-derived every element from the primary sources and built my own probe rather than trusting the offered evidence.

**The quotes are accurate.** Verified verbatim at ADR-021:125-129 (D3's rationale: "the day someone adds `MC_HOME/quarantine/`, an enumerated-only rule permits it and no test fails. A whole-tree rule cannot drift."), :350-353 (union-membership tests: the fifteen classes, never MC_HOME itself), :376-379 (planted mutants: "identity→prefix comparison; ancestor direction removed; `broad_root` as a literal `/Users` list; jurisdiction after `ResolveAccess`; `denied_paths` made subtractable" — no whole-tree mutant), and :131-133 (the "but only if `sessions/` exists" concession). A grep of the entire Tests+Mutants section (ADR-021:345-392) for "zero|quarantine|whole|unenumerated|tree" returns no hits: neither D3's whole-tree root nor D1's zero-value law is pinned.

**In scope, not re-litigation.** The extract's gap (f) confirms ADR-017 leaves the whole-vs-enumerated question open ("Is `MC_HOME` protected in whole, or only its enumerated roots?"). D3 is ADR-021's own resolution, so judging its test coverage is exactly what this review is for. The finding does not dispute D3's decision — it endorses it and says the test list fails to pin it.

**Reachability — the finding survives the test that kills most plausible findings.** I checked the blocked floor, the likeliest upstream rule that would make the state unconstructible: `blocked.go:28-69` contains no MC_HOME pattern; `quarantine` matches no `PatternComponent` and no `PatternBasenameGlob`. Step 3 does not catch it. ADR-017's own text does not force the whole-tree root either (its union at :366-386 enumerates) — which is precisely why gap (f) exists. Nothing upstream blocks the mutant.

**My probe (`mc/boundary/probe_d3_test.go`, `mise exec -- go test ./boundary/ -run TestProbe_D3Mutant -v`)** implements D4-faithful bidirectional `os.SameFile` identity comparison and drives every fixture named at ADR-021:349-379 against an enumeration-only mutant. All 23 pass — mutant not killed by any. `Rejects(MC_HOME/quarantine)` under D3-faithful = "descendant of protected root .../mc-home"; under the mutant = `<nil>`.

**My probe sharpens the finding beyond what was offered.** The original evidence stops at "sibling `MC_HOME/sessions/other` rejects". I additionally drove `MC_HOME` *itself*: it **rejects under the mutant** (reject=true), because D4's ancestor walk catches it as an ancestor of the enumerated `sessions/`. So even the obvious defensive test — "mounting MC_HOME rejects" — does *not* kill this mutant. The **only** witness that kills it is a child of MC_HOME that is neither ancestor nor descendant of any enumerated class, and that is the exact fixture the list omits. This makes the gap harder to stumble into than the finding claims.

**Severity: minor, agreeing with the reviewer's own claim.** It is a real, demonstrated, bounded test-coverage defect, not a design error: D3's prose at :121-122 is normative and unambiguous, and Sharp edge 2 (:325-330) explicitly warns against removing it. An implementer following the ADR builds the correct rule. The escape requires an implementer to violate explicit ADR text — the tests merely fail to catch them when they do. Nothing escapes today; nothing is unimplementable. It does not rise to major. But it is not cosmetic either: AGENTS.md §3 makes the test set the acceptance criteria, and this ADR's own methodology (":376-379, Each must die with an exercised witness") is the designated guard against exactly this drift — D3's rationale describes a mutant its own suite does not kill.

The D1 zero-value sub-claim also holds (:86-88 states the law; no test or mutant at :347-379). One correction to its framing: the `BlockPolicy` precedent it invokes is itself untested — `grep "BlockPolicy{}" mc/boundary/*_test.go` returns nothing — so this is a pre-existing pattern in the package, not a regression against a tested precedent. That makes it a weaker sub-point than the D3 gap, though still a genuine omission.
verdicts.recommended_action:
Two cheap additions to ADR-021's "Tests that pin it" section, before the slice is built TDD-first:

1. **Add the missing union-membership fixture** at :349-353: an **unenumerated `MC_HOME` child** (e.g. `MC_HOME/quarantine`) rejects when allowlisted. Critically — per my probe — the fixture must be a path that is neither an ancestor nor a descendant of any enumerated class; a test that mounts `MC_HOME` itself does NOT work, because D4's ancestor direction catches it via `sessions/` and the enumeration-only mutant survives it. This is the only witness that kills the mutant.

2. **Add "whole-tree `MC_HOME` root removed" to the planted-mutant list** at :376-379, so D3's own rationale ("no test fails") is falsified by the suite it ships with rather than restated by it.

3. Lower priority: add a test pinning D1's zero-value law (:86-88) — a zero `Jurisdiction` rejects everything — and name "zero `Jurisdiction` permits everything" as a mutant. Note this is a pre-existing package pattern (`BlockPolicy`'s zero-value law at `blocked.go:72-76` is likewise untested), so it may reasonably be handled as a separate cleanup rather than swept into this slice.

None of this changes a decision — D3 and D1 stay exactly as written. It is purely making the ADR's stated acceptance criteria actually pin its two most consequential laws. Probe left at `mc/boundary/probe_d3_test.go` in the review worktree as the reproduction; it is scaffolding, not code to merge.

verdicts.reasoning:
**What I read and ran myself.**

1. **The ADR text is as quoted.** `docs/adr/021-mount-jurisdiction.md:76-79` (D1): "`JurisdictionInput` carries **already-canonical identities** (`device`, `inode`, `type`), resolved by the caller — the host side — from canonical spine records. The package never queries the spine, the Git registry, or the environment." No scope qualifier — it is a flat statement about the whole input. `:104-105` (D2): the 512 bound "is checked **before any `os.Stat` or identity walk**", inside `ResolveJurisdiction`, which `:97` calls "the **only** constructor". `:268-271` (D8): absent members "resolve the nearest existing canonical ancestor, retain the unresolved suffix". `:335-336` (Consequence 4): "Building it is host-side work with real `os.Stat` cost at every spawn." `:347` heads the test list "Fast lane, red-first, `mc/boundary`:" and `:356-357` / `:372-373` place "512+1 deny paths rejects before any stat" and "a non-existent deny path resolves through its nearest existing ancestor" inside that package. I grepped `mc/` for `JurisdictionInput|ResolveJurisdiction|Jurisdiction` — zero hits, so the type exists only as the identifier in the D1 signature block. The ADR never defines its fields.

2. **The two readings are not reconcilable, and one of them violates ADR-017.** ADR-017:167-169 verbatim: "One Sandbox Profile has at most 64 artifact roots, 128 readonly mounts, and 512 denied paths. **A boundary excess rejects before identity walking or allocation**; none of these collections is truncated." Under D1's literal reading the host resolves all 513 `denied_paths` *before* `ResolveJurisdiction` is entered, so the bound check D2 places in the constructor is provably too late and ADR-017:168 is broken — while D2 forbids a second constructor that could check it earlier. Under the other reading (`ResolveJurisdiction` takes paths and stats them, as `ResolveAllowlist` already does via `ResolveSource`, `identity.go:173-181`), D1:76-79 and Consequence 4's "host-side `os.Stat` cost" are simply false. A mixed input (raw `denied_paths` + pre-resolved MC members) would satisfy both, but the ADR never says that, and D8:275-281 explicitly extends nearest-existing-ancestor resolution to "**Every other absent member** (an `MC_HOME/seals` that does not exist yet, an other-Worksource artifact root that does not)" — i.e. to the MC-derived side too, which pre-resolution cannot express.

3. **Probe (the part I did not take on trust).** D4:146 says "Every comparison is `os.SameFile` on resolved objects". I wrote a probe in `mc/boundary` giving `os.SameFile` a `fs.FileInfo` carrying only a `(device, inode, type)` triple — exactly what D1 says the input carries — and ran `mise exec -- go test ./boundary/ -run TestProbeSameFile -v`:
```
SameFile(realStat, synthetic)       = false
SameFile(synthetic, synthetic)      = false
SameFile(realStat, realStat)        = true
```
`os.SameFile` type-asserts both arguments to the unexported `*os.fileStat` and returns **false** for anything else — silently. So D1's stated input type composed with D4's stated mechanism does not merely under-specify; it **fails open**: no protected root ever matches. Reading 1 is literally unimplementable as written unless `mc/boundary` re-stats, which is what D1 forbids and D2's "before any `os.Stat`" presumes it does. (I removed the probe; `go build ./...` is green and `git status --porcelain` is clean.)

4. **Not blocked by an upstream rule.** The extract's §8(g) states the open gap as "must arrive **pre-resolved**, which means defining a jurisdiction input type carrying canonicalized identities. **The ADR never draws that seam**", and §8(i) leaves absent-member handling open. ADR-021 D1 answers (g) in the pre-resolved direction and D8 answers (i) in a direction that needs the filesystem; nothing in ADR-017, the blocked floor (`blocked.go`), or the spec makes the conflict unconstructible — there is no CHECK here at all, because there is no code here at all. `phase3-contract.md:169`'s "shared `mc/boundary` policy used by profile save and both sides of `mc dispatch`" is real and is what makes the placement of the bound check load-bearing rather than cosmetic.

5. **Not re-litigation.** The finding does not dispute any ADR-017 or operator decision. ADR-017:167-169 and :275-276 are taken as given; the defect is entirely in whether ADR-021 correctly and unambiguously resolves what ADR-017 left open at gaps (g)/(h)/(i). Defining this seam is ADR-021's own stated principal contribution ("it defines the seam and the slice after it fills it", :386-387).

**Where I trim the finding.** The reviewer's fail-open branch would be caught by the ADR's own union-membership tests (:349-353), so it is not a shippable escape. But the 512-bound and D8 placement divergence is caught by nothing — an implementer who moves both host-side simply never writes the two fast-lane tests the ADR lists, and ADR-017:168's ordering rule then lives in un-ADR'd host code. That is the durable half of the finding, and it stands.

**Severity.** Major: D1's flat statement of the input type is a load-bearing claim that the ADR's own D2, D8, and fast-lane test list falsify, and the literal composition of D1 with D4 is unimplementable (probe above). The ADR's central deliverable — the seam — is left with two wire-incompatible readings that differ in whether an accepted ADR's bound-ordering rule is honoured.
verdicts.recommended_action: Before TDD, pin `JurisdictionInput`'s shape in D1 with an actual Go struct, not prose. The conservative resolution (AGENTS.md §6) is the mixed input the rest of the ADR already implies: `denied_paths []string` — raw, operator-derived, unresolved, so `ResolveJurisdiction` can check the 512 bound before it stats anything and thereby honour ADR-017:168 inside the only constructor — plus MC-derived members arriving as host-resolved values. Then fix three consequences of that choice: (1) state that the resolved members carry an `fs.FileInfo` obtained from a host `os.Stat`, never a bare `(device, inode, type)` triple — `os.SameFile` returns false for any FileInfo the `os` package did not produce (probe above), so a triple silently disables D4's entire mechanism; (2) give D8's absent-member case an explicit representation in the type — an `(ancestorInfo fs.FileInfo, unresolvedSuffix string)` pair — and say which side computes it, since D8:275-281 extends the rule to MC-derived members that pre-resolution cannot represent; (3) reconcile D1's "the package never queries ... the environment" and the warm-helper warning with the fact that D4's ancestor walk and D8's resolution both require `os.Stat` inside `mc/boundary`, exactly as `enclosesByIdentity` (`identity.go:198-210`) and `match` (`identity.go:284-303`) already do — "pure" here must be defined as "no spine/Git-registry/env access", not "no filesystem", or the two rules cannot coexist. Also correct D1:80-82's cite: the "warm helper cannot see unmounted host Worksource paths" warning is at `phase3-contract.md:46-47`, not :41.
